product_title: Tech Debt Audit Prompt Pack for Claude Code
rationale: A Claude Code skill for thorough, file-cited tech debt audits is trending and no existing product in the published list covers tech debt analysis. Freelance developers and dev team leads face tech debt audit requests constantly and need structured prompts to produce credible, actionable reports.
plan_prompt: /plan Build a 20-prompt pack titled 'Tech Debt Audit Prompt Pack for Claude Code' priced at $19 on Gumroad. Audience: freelance developers, tech leads, and engineering consultants who need to audit codebases for tech debt and communicate findings clearly to clients or stakeholders. Product type: prompt-pack. Structure in 5 sections: (1) Codebase Discovery (4 prompts: generate file-by-file tech debt inventory, identify dead code and unused dependencies, map circular dependencies and tight coupling, surface outdated library versions with CVE risk), (2) Code Quality Analysis (4 prompts: audit for missing error handling patterns, identify duplicated logic and DRY violations, flag overly complex functions by cyclomatic complexity, find inconsistent naming and style violations), (3) Architecture Debt (4 prompts: identify architectural anti-patterns, surface missing abstraction layers, flag scalability bottlenecks in data access patterns, identify missing or weak test coverage by module), (4) Prioritization and Roadmap (4 prompts: score debt items by effort vs. risk matrix, generate a phased remediation roadmap, write executive summary of debt severity, create a 30/60/90 day debt paydown plan), (5) Stakeholder Communication (4 prompts: write client-facing tech debt report, generate engineering team debt retrospective agenda, create debt tracking ticket templates, write justification for debt remediation budget). Each prompt copy-paste ready for Claude Code with [codebase], [tech stack], and [audience] placeholders. Tone: authoritative, engineer-credible, client-presentable. Selling angle: turn any codebase into a documented, prioritized tech debt audit in hours instead of days.

product_title: TDD Feedback Loop Prompt Pack for Claude Code
rationale: A 16-skill TDD-driven development loop (brainstorm → plan → execute → iterate) is a genuinely novel workflow pattern not covered by any existing product in the published list. Freelance developers using Claude Code need structured prompts for disciplined iterative development, not just one-off code generation.
plan_prompt: /plan Build a 20-prompt pack titled 'TDD Feedback Loop Prompt Pack for Claude Code' priced at $19 on Gumroad. Audience: freelance developers and indie hackers using Claude Code who want to move from ad-hoc AI coding to a disciplined test-driven, iterative development workflow. Product type: prompt-pack. Structure in 5 sections mirroring the TDD loop: (1) Brainstorm and Scope (4 prompts: generate user stories from rough idea, identify edge cases before writing code, define acceptance criteria from feature description, create minimal viable scope from requirements), (2) Plan and Architect (4 prompts: generate implementation plan with file structure, identify dependencies and risks before coding, write pseudocode for complex function, create checkpoint milestones for iterative build), (3) Test-First Writing (4 prompts: write failing unit tests before implementation, generate test cases from acceptance criteria, write integration test stubs for new feature, create mock data for test scenarios), (4) Execute and Implement (4 prompts: implement code to pass existing tests, refactor after green tests, handle edge cases identified in tests, generate error handling from test failure patterns), (5) Review and Iterate (4 prompts: run post-implementation code review checklist, identify next iteration improvements, generate regression test additions, write commit message and PR description from changes). Each prompt copy-paste ready with [placeholders]. Tone: disciplined, developer-focused, respects TDD principles. Selling angle: stop writing throwaway code — use Claude Code as a true TDD partner with structured prompts for every phase of the loop.

rationale: Running a persistent, always-on Claude Code agent with browser capabilities on your own machine is a genuinely new workflow pattern with no existing product in the published list. Product type: mini-guide. Structure: (1) What a 24/7 Agent Actually Means (difference between one-shot AI and persistent agent, what 'on any box you own' unlocks vs. cloud-only agents, 150 words), (2) Hardware and OS Requirements (minimum specs, recommended Linux/Mac setup, Docker vs. bare metal trade-offs, 150 words), (3) Installing and Configuring Claude Code for Agent Mode (API key setup, agent permission scopes, browser harness installation, 200 words), (4) Defining Agent Tasks and Goals (how to write persistent task files, setting success criteria, configuring retry and error recovery behavior, 200 words), (5) Keeping the Agent Running (systemd service vs. screen/tmux vs. Docker restart policy, monitoring agent health, log rotation, 150 words), (6) Safety and Cost Controls (rate limiting API calls, setting spend caps, sandboxing browser access, what to never let an agent do unsupervised, 150 words), (7) Quick-Start Checklist (10 action items to go from zero to running agent in one afternoon, 100 words). Tone: pragmatic, security-conscious, developer-friendly. Selling angle: stop babysitting your AI — configure a persistent agent that works while you sleep, safely, on hardware you control.

rationale: Persistent memory for AI agents (episodes, facts, working context via Postgres + MCP) is a real gap developers face when building production agents — no existing product covers this topic in the published list. Structure: (1) Why Agent Memory Matters (the stateless problem — why agents forget, and what it costs in UX and reliability, 150 words), (2) Three Types of Agent Memory (episodic/conversational history, factual/entity memory, working context — explain each with one real example, 200 words), (3) Architecture Options (self-hosted Postgres approach vs. hosted vector DBs vs. simple file-based memory, with trade-offs, 200 words), (4) MCP Server Integration (what MCP is, how to connect a memory layer to Claude Code or similar via MCP, step-by-step overview, 250 words), (5) What to Store and What to Forget (memory hygiene — TTLs, relevance scoring, avoiding context bloat, 150 words), (6) Quick-Start Checklist (10 action items to get a working memory layer in one afternoon, 150 words). Tone: pragmatic, developer-friendly, no academic jargon. Selling angle: stop building amnesiac agents — add persistent memory in an afternoon without a cloud dependency.

product_title: Database Client Setup Checklist for Developers
rationale: Open-source multi-database GUI client trending signals freelance developers setting up local database environments — a practical setup + configuration checklist for multi-DB environments (MySQL, Postgres, MongoDB, Redis) has no equivalent in the existing product list and fills a real repeated pain point.
plan_prompt: /plan Build a 20-step checklist titled 'Multi-Database Setup and Configuration Checklist for Freelance Developers' priced at $9 on Gumroad. Audience: freelance developers and consultants who need to quickly set up, connect, and manage multiple database types (MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQLite, Redis, MongoDB) for client projects using lightweight GUI or CLI tools. Product type: checklist. Structure in 5 sections: (1) Environment Prep (4 steps: install database engines locally vs. Docker, set up environment variables for connection strings, configure firewall/network rules for local access, create .env template for multi-DB project), (2) Connection Setup Per DB Type (4 steps: connect MySQL/Postgres with role-based credentials, configure MongoDB connection string with auth, set Redis connection with optional TLS, test all connections with a health-check query), (3) Security Hardening (4 steps: disable remote root login, rotate credentials before client handoff, enable SSL/TLS for production connections, set up read-only replica credentials for reporting), (4) Query and Management Workflow (4 steps: set up saved queries library, configure query history and audit logging, set up scheduled backup exports, document schema version for client), (5) Handoff and Documentation (4 steps: generate connection config summary doc for client, write database access runbook, create offboarding credential revocation checklist, tag and archive schema snapshots). Tone: practical, checklist-native, no assumed expertise. Selling angle: never forget a database config step on a client project again — every DB type covered in one checklist.
